title: Extending the life of clothing and textiles
subtitle: Repair, resale, donation, and swap routes before clothing reaches landfill
excerpt: The UK sends 350,000 tonnes of clothing to landfill every year. Most garments that are discarded are still wearable. A structured approach to clothing moves usable items through resale, swapping, or donation before they reach the bin. Items past repair are collected by textile banks for fibre recycling. Very little clothing needs to go to landfill.
sourceNotes: WRAP Valuing Our Clothes report (2020). Ellen MacArthur Foundation A New Textiles Economy (2017). Oxfam clothing waste research (2023). TRAID UK textile collection data.

content.text: A garment discarded after seven wears has roughly three times the carbon footprint per use of one worn 30 times. Extending the wearing life is the most effective reduction step available. The route after that is resale or donation, which displaces new purchases elsewhere. Fibre recycling is the last resort and recovers only a fraction of the original material value.
Most clothing failures are fixable: missing buttons, split seams, broken zips, and worn-through elbows. A button is a 5-minute repair. A zip replacement is 20 to 30 minutes.
Sew it Yourself (SIY) culture: basic hand-sewing repairs require no machine. Keep a needle, thread in neutral colours, and spare buttons.
Repair cafes: local volunteer repair cafes can fix more complex problems (shoe repairs, lining replacement, alterations). Find one at repaircafe.org.

Vinted, Depop, and eBay: effective for garments in good condition. Photograph against a plain background in natural light. List the measurements as well as the size label, since sizing varies between brands.
Dress agencies and consignment shops: the shop sells the item and takes a commission (typically 40 to 50 percent). Less effort than direct selling; suits higher-value items.
Batch selling: listing 10 to 15 items together makes the effort worthwhile. A monthly clear-out and listing session is more effective than listing one item at a time.
Charity shops: accept most clean, wearable clothing. Check the specific shop's guidelines. Some do not accept school uniforms, workwear, or heavily pilled items.

Textile banks (in supermarket car parks): accept items not suitable for resale, such as heavily worn, stained, or torn clothing. These go to sorting facilities for fibre processing or industrial rags.
Do not donate damaged or unwearable items to charity shops. It costs charities staff time and disposal fees to process them. Textile banks are the right route for unusable items.

Clothing swaps at community centres, through local Facebook groups, or at organised swap events let participants exchange items directly. No money changes hands. The model works best for children's clothing (where sizing is outgrown, not worn out) and for seasonal items that one person has used once and another needs.
